---
title: "02 · Topology — Open Sets, Continuity, Compactness"
subtitle: "Geometry without distance — and the right level of abstraction for continuity"
lesson-id: "t7.topology"
tier: 7
prerequisites: ["t5.real-analysis-foundation"]
unlocks: []
applied-domains: ["research"]
estimated-minutes: 25
---

::: {.preflight-check}
### Pre-flight check {.mt-0}

1. Have you done [T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)?
2. Comfortable with $\epsilon$-$\delta$ continuity?
3. Recall sequences and convergence.
:::

## Touch

A **topological space** $(X, \tau)$: $X$ is a set, $\tau$ is a
collection of "open" subsets satisfying:

- $\emptyset, X \in \tau$.
- $\tau$ closed under arbitrary unions and finite intersections.

```{ojs}
viewof topSel = Inputs.select(["Standard topology on ℝ", "Discrete: every set is open", "Trivial: only ∅ and X open", "Subspace: induced from a parent"], {value: "Standard topology on ℝ", label: "Topology:"})
```

```{ojs}
{
  let info;
  switch(topSel) {
    case "Standard topology on ℝ": info = "Open sets = unions of open intervals. Familiar topology of the real line."; break;
    case "Discrete: every set is open": info = "Most refined topology. Every function is continuous. Useless for analysis but conceptually instructive."; break;
    case "Trivial: only ∅ and X open": info = "Most coarse. Only constant functions are continuous to most spaces."; break;
    case "Subspace: induced from a parent": info = "Y ⊆ X gets topology {Y ∩ U : U open in X}. The natural inheritance."; break;
  }
  return html`<div style="margin: 1rem 0;">
    <div style="font-family:monospace; font-size:0.95rem; color:#c2185b; text-align:center;">${info}</div>
  </div>`;
}
```

## Wonder

1. Why generalise beyond metric spaces?
2. **Continuity** in topology: $f^{-1}(\text{open}) = \text{open}$.
   Why does this match the $\epsilon$-$\delta$ definition?
3. **Compactness** is the topological version of "bounded and
   closed" — but more general.

## Discover

### Problem 1

A **topological space** $(X, \tau)$: $\tau \subseteq 2^X$ contains
$\emptyset, X$, is closed under arbitrary unions and finite
intersections.

For $\mathbb{R}$ with the standard topology, is $[0, 1]$ open?
Closed?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Closed but not open** in standard topology. $[0, 1] \subset
\mathbb{R}$ has boundary points $0, 1$ that are not interior — so
not open. Its complement $\mathbb{R} \setminus [0, 1] = (-\infty, 0)
\cup (1, \infty)$ is open — so $[0, 1]$ is closed.

Sets can be open, closed, both ($\emptyset, X$), or neither (e.g.,
$[0, 1)$).
</details>

### Problem 2

**Continuity, topological version.** $f: X \to Y$ is continuous iff
$f^{-1}(V)$ is open in $X$ for every open $V \subseteq Y$.

Verify: for $\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$, this matches
$\epsilon$-$\delta$.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
$f$ $\epsilon$-$\delta$-continuous at $a$: for every open ball $B(f(a),
\epsilon)$ around $f(a)$, $f^{-1}$ contains an open ball $B(a,
\delta)$.

Topologically: $f^{-1}$ of any open set is open — exactly the same
information, just phrased without explicit metrics. The
*topological* definition extends to spaces without distance
(quotient spaces, product spaces).
</details>

### Problem 3

A **homeomorphism** $f: X \to Y$ is a continuous bijection with
continuous inverse. Topologically, $X$ and $Y$ are "the same."

Are $(0, 1)$ and $\mathbb{R}$ homeomorphic?

<details class="hint"><summary>Hint 1 — a nudge</summary>
$f(x) = \tan(\pi x - \pi/2)$ maps $(0, 1)$ continuously, bijectively
to $\mathbb{R}$, with continuous inverse $\arctan$.
</details>
<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Yes, homeomorphic.** Topology can't distinguish "bounded" from
"unbounded" intervals.

But: $(0, 1)$ and $[0, 1]$ are *not* homeomorphic. The latter is
**compact**, the former isn't.
</details>

### Problem 4

**Compactness.** $X$ is compact iff every open cover has a finite
subcover.

For $\mathbb{R}^n$: compact iff closed and bounded (Heine-Borel).
For general spaces: compactness is a deep property.

Why is compactness so useful?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Compactness lets you reduce infinite to finite.** Continuous
functions on compact spaces:

- Attain their max and min (extreme value theorem).
- Are uniformly continuous.

Compact sets in metric spaces are *complete and totally bounded* —
they "almost behave like finite sets."

In analysis: a sequence in a compact set has a convergent
subsequence (Bolzano-Weierstrass).

In algebra: compact groups have powerful representation theory.
</details>

### Problem 5

**Connectedness.** $X$ is connected iff it cannot be written as a
disjoint union of two non-empty open sets.

Is $[0, 1] \cup [2, 3] \subset \mathbb{R}$ connected? Is the unit
disk?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**$[0, 1] \cup [2, 3]$:** disconnected — splits into two open-in-
subspace pieces.

**Unit disk:** connected — can't split.

The **fundamental group** $\pi_1$ measures "holes" in connected
spaces — the discipline of *algebraic topology* (Tier 7+).

Connectedness explains: why intermediate value theorem holds
(continuous image of connected is connected), why some equations
must have solutions.
</details>

## Name

::: {.lesson-beat}

A **topological space** $(X, \tau)$: $\tau$ a collection of subsets
("opens") containing $\emptyset, X$, closed under arbitrary unions
and finite intersections.

**Closed sets** = complements of open sets.

**Continuous function** $f: X \to Y$: $f^{-1}(\text{open}) = \text{open}$.

**Homeomorphism:** continuous bijection with continuous inverse.
Topological "sameness."

**Compactness:** every open cover has a finite subcover.

- In $\mathbb{R}^n$: compact ⇔ closed and bounded (Heine-Borel).
- Continuous image of compact is compact.
- Continuous functions on compact spaces attain max/min.

**Connectedness:** can't split into disjoint non-empty opens.

**Path-connected:** every pair connected by a continuous path. Path-
connected ⇒ connected (converse not always).

**Hausdorff (T2):** distinct points have disjoint neighbourhoods.
"Reasonable" spaces (most analysis happens here).

**Common topologies:**

- Discrete (all subsets open).
- Trivial (only $\emptyset, X$).
- Standard on $\mathbb{R}^n$ (open balls).
- Product, subspace, quotient topologies.

:::

> *Topology = sets + open subsets, closed under unions/finite intersections;*
> *Continuous: pre-image of open is open;*
> *Compact = "tame infinity"; connected = "one piece".*

## Apply

### Worked example

> Show that the function $f: \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}, f(x) = x^3$ is
> a homeomorphism.

Continuous (polynomial). Bijective ($f$ strictly increasing).

Inverse $f^{-1}(y) = y^{1/3}$ — continuous on $\mathbb{R}$.

So $\mathbb{R} \cong \mathbb{R}$ (trivially) via $x \mapsto x^3$. (More
interesting: $f(x) = x^3$ is a homeomorphism even though it's not
*differentiably* invertible at 0.)

### Practice — Warm

**W1.** Is $(0, 1)$ open in $\mathbb{R}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes.
</details>

**W2.** Is $\mathbb{Z}$ closed in $\mathbb{R}$?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes — its complement (union of open intervals) is open.
</details>

**W3.** Is the unit sphere $S^2$ in $\mathbb{R}^3$ compact?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Yes (closed and bounded).
</details>

### Practice — Working

**P1.** Is the open interval $(0, \infty)$ compact?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No** — unbounded.
</details>

**P2.** Is the rationals $\mathbb{Q}$ in $\mathbb{R}$ open? Closed?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Neither.** Not open (no interval is fully rational). Not closed
(its closure is $\mathbb{R}$).
</details>

**P3.** Are the unit circle $S^1$ and unit interval $[0, 1]$
homeomorphic?

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**No.** Removing one point from $S^1$ keeps it connected; from $[0,
1]$, splitting at an interior point disconnects it. Topological
invariant differs.
</details>

### Practice — Stretch

**S1.** **Topological data analysis.** Persistent homology computes
topological features (connected components, holes, voids) of point
clouds at multiple scales. Used in ML for shape analysis.

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
Given a point cloud, build a *Vietoris-Rips complex* at each scale
$\epsilon$; track which holes appear and disappear as $\epsilon$
grows. Long-persisting features = real structure; short ones =
noise.

Applications: drug discovery, sensor networks, neural network
analysis. The mathematics rests squarely on Tier 7+ algebraic
topology.
</details>

**S2.** **Why is topology pre-foundational for many fields?**

<details class="hint"><summary>Full solution</summary>
**Topology supplies the bare-minimum framework for continuity.**
Wherever you have a notion of "limit" or "near", topology applies.

Examples:

- Real and complex analysis (continuity, compactness).
- Functional analysis (topologies on infinite-dim spaces).
- Algebraic geometry (Zariski topology).
- Quantum field theory (manifolds with boundary).
- Computer science (Scott topology for domains in semantics).

The minimal axioms make it generalise everywhere.
</details>

::: {.common-confusion}
### Common confusions

1. **"Open and closed are opposites."** A set can be both ($\emptyset,
   X$) or neither ($[0, 1)$ in $\mathbb{R}$).
2. **"Compact = bounded."** True in $\mathbb{R}^n$ (Heine-Borel) but
   not in general.
3. **"All topologies are useful."** Discrete and trivial topologies
   are mostly pedagogical; standard, product, quotient, and Zariski
   are practical workhorses.
:::

### Self-Assessment

1. Could you state the topological definition of continuity?
2. Without scrolling back, what's compactness?
3. Did you solve at least 4 of the 5 *Discover* problems and 4 of the 6
   practice problems without peeking past *Hint 1*?

(Answer: every open cover has a finite subcover.)

- **3 yes:** ready for **[03 · Galois Theory](../03-galois-theory/index.qmd)**.
- **2 yes:** redo **S1** (TDA).
- **0–1 yes:** re-read *Discover P3 and P4*.

## Connect

### Prerequisites used

- **[T5 L15 — Real Analysis Foundation](../../tier-5-undergrad-core/15-real-analysis-foundation/index.qmd)**

### What this unlocks

- Algebraic topology, differential topology, manifold theory.

### Where this matters

::: {.applied-callout}
**Mathematical research.** *The* foundation of analysis, algebraic
geometry, and many branches of pure math.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**AI / Machine Learning.** Topological data analysis (S1).
Manifold learning. Theoretical analysis of neural network landscapes.
:::

::: {.applied-callout}
**Theoretical computer science.** Topological semantics of
programming languages, domain theory, knot theory in DNA computing.
:::
